entry.title: Ked - Kannada Editor
entry.content:
A Kannada Editor for English Keyboards. website

When making subtitles/titles for yakshagana videos, I face this constant problem of writing kannada using english keyboards. Most of the online solutions transliterate by sending the word you’ve typed in english to google translate and suggesting the closest kannada word in the kannada script.

This is an OK solution, but many a time, it is not accurate, it struggles with old kannada words. It also does not provide character level control over the script, which makes it very hard to edit incorrect suggestions.

One more usecase (which interests me in particular) is writing Tulu in the kannada script. Tulu has viramas in the middle of words. For example, take the phrase ಶುದ್ಧ ಮನಸ್‌ಡ್. There is a virama after the ಸ. This is very difficult to write with any of the existing online editors. In unicode, you would need to use the ZWNJ (U+200C) to ensure that the ಸ and ಡ don’t get combined into a ottu akshara.


Here’s what I did:

  • Simple key map from english letters -> kannada letters.
  • key map is always on display for reference.
  • \ is used to represent a forced virama, which will help writing tulu.
  • Here are a few examples:
    • manas\D -> ಮನಸ್‌ಡ್
    • pRthvi -> ಪೃಥ್ವಿ
    • kannaDa -> ಕನ್ನಡ
    • kaartaviiryaarjuna -> ಕಾರ್ತವೀರ್ಯಾರ್ಜುನ


Here’s what it looks like, try it out, let me know if it helps!
entry.summary: A Kannada Editor for English Keyboards. website

entry.title: Yakshagana Prasanga Prathi Library
entry.content:
A Yakshagana Prasanga Prathi Library. website

Main source of data for this project is from the people at Yakshavahini. Somehow, it seems they have co-ordinated with a lot of yakshagana enjoyers and digitized upwards of 1700 parasanga prathis! Uncs have uploaded everything to archive.org and google drive. Yakshavahini have also published their own version of cleaned up prathis. Lot of great work!

I’ve just put everything in one place and created a faster search on top of it. It works with both Kannada and English. Here’s a video tutorial about how to use the website. It’s pretty self explanatory though.
entry.summary: A Yakshagana Prasanga Prathi Library. website

entry.title: Yakshagana Raga Library
entry.content:
A Yakshagana Raga reference website. website

The main source I used is this awesome video from Diwanagraphy. It’s a compilation of many songs from Kalladka Chinmaya Bhat. Mainly this is a reference for myself. Made it literally in a day with claude.
entry.summary: A Yakshagana Raga reference website. website

entry.title: Costal Karnataka Brahmins - Kuladevaru and Gotra

link.title: Costal Karnataka Brahmins - Kuladevaru and Gotra

entry.content:
Recently I got access to a dataset about costal karnataka brahmins. Here are some interesting findings.

There are mainly five types of brahmins that I’ve analyzed.

  • Kandavara Smartha
  • Kota Smartha
  • Koteshwara Madhwa
  • Shivalli Smartha
  • Shivalli Madhwa

Kandavara Smartha

N=22 These are brahmins who settled in Kandavara. (near Kundapura) They are followers of Adi Shankaracharya.

Vishwamitra is the most common gotra among them:

Kuladevaru:

  • Bharadwaja - Anantheshwara, Udupi
  • Jamadagni - Kunjarugiri
  • Kashyapa - Ulluru Subramanya
  • Viswamitra - Ulluru Subramanya
  • Haritasa - Anatheshwara & Ulluru

Kota Smartha

N=234 These are brahmins who settled in Kota. (Saligrama) They are also followers of Adi Shankaracharya (i.e. Smartha)

Vishwamitra is the most common gotra here as well:

Koundinya N=1, Haritasa N=2 are probably inaccuracies in the data and not gotras that are present in the community.

All people from vishwamitra gotra have pravara as Madhushchanda / Dhananjaya. Devarata / Oudala pravara is not seen in Kota smartha brahmins.

Kuladevaru:

  • Guru Narasimha, Saligrama for all gotras.

Koteshwara Madhwa

N=107 These are brahmins who settled in Koteshwara. (Kundapura) They are followers of Madhwacharya.

More than half of the population have gotra as Vishwamitra:

Kuladevaru:

  • Angirasa - Guru Narasimha, Saligrama
  • Vashishta - Guru Narasimha, Saligrama
  • Vishwamitra - Mix of mainly Saligrama (60%) & Neelavara (23%)

Shivalli Brahmins

There are mainly two categories:

  • Shivalli Smartha
  • Shivalli Madhwa

Shivalli Madhwa comprise majority of the population:

Smartha are kannada speaking and Madhwa are Tulu speaking. There are a few N=6 Shivalli Madhwa kannada speaking brahmins. These are most likely people who lost the ability to speak tulu.

Shivalli Madhwa

N=925 These brahmins settled in a place called Shivalli near Udupi. They are followers of Madhwacharya.

Unlike most other cases we have seen so far, Angirasa is the most common gotra!

Kuladevaru:

  • Angirasa
    • Alevooru Vishnumoorthy (41%)
    • Kodangala Vishnumoorthy (15%)
  • Bhargava
    • Inna Muddannu, Mundkur (86%)
  • Vishwamitra
    • Neelavara Mahishamardhini (45%)
    • Ulluru Subramanya (39%)

Vishwamitra Gotra has mainly 2 pravaras. Devarata/Audala for whom the Kuladevaru is Mahishamardhini, Neelavara and Dhananjaya/Madhuschanda for whom the Kuladevaru is Ulluru Subramanya.

Shivalli Smartha

N=230 These brahmins continued to remain followers of Adi Shankaracharya ever after Madhwacharya rose to prominence in the area.

There is no major bias seen in the gotra wise distribution:

Kuladevaru:

  • Vishwamitra
    • Ulluru Subramanya (57%)
    • Neelavara Mahishamardhini (15%)
  • Haritasa
    • Udupi Anantheshwara (59%)
    • Kunjargiri Durgaparameshwari (13%)
    • Alevoor Vishnumoorthy (9%)
    • Others (19%)
  • Bharadwaja
    • Udupi Anantheshwara (58%)
    • Shri. Shankaranarayana (10%)


Data used is from a Shivalli brahmin focused matrimony service. An anonymized version of the dataset for public consumption can be found here.
entry.summary: Recently I got access to a dataset about costal karnataka brahmins. Here are some interesting findings.
